observations.subject: literacy
observations.transcription: Okay, let's start. Uh, I will show you some pictures, and you have to guess what that picture is. Okay? You have to tell me what that picture is. Okay? First of all, let's see what this picture is. What picture is this? What do you understand by looking at it? A book. A book? Which book? Language. English? What about English? Okay, fine. It's an English book. Which book? Look at it—just see, there are dragons on it, and things like that—where do they appear in books? And think of something else. What do you study? Which other ones? A storybook. A storybook. Who said this? A storybook. You're studying by looking at it. These are storybooks, tales, fairy tales. So where do books like this exist? In fairy tales, and so on. Very good. Now there is another picture. Now there is another picture again. What happens in storybooks? There's acting, and you have some characters, in which they have acted differently, and you read them differently. Actually, there aren't dragons and such, there aren't fairies and such, but they're in your storybooks. Okay? Alright, there's another picture. Now you have to tell me by looking at it what this is. Like this—these things, what are they used for? Machines. Machines? It's a machine. And this—what is this? This is also used in machines, right? What are these too? Machines. Okay, fine. What is this too? A kind of machine. This—uh—my mobile, what is this too? A machine. So what is a machine? Basically, anything that helps you in daily life. Okay? No—a washing machine isn't for washing clothes. My mobile doesn't wash clothes. But what is this too? A machine. What makes my daily life easier, what is that? A machine. Okay? Alright, look at another picture. Now look at this—or all the kids, look carefully. What are they doing? All the kids, everyone is together, working on something together, right? Wait, just a moment. Oh! Okay, so everyone is just working together. When you do some work together, what do you call it? Look at this. Yes, working together. Like this, when I give you in a group, they also have a word. Does anyone know what that is? Making things. Hmm, how? When you say, "We've formed a group together," what does that mean? You end up forming a team, right? What do you have? A team. So whenever you do some work together, what do we call it? Teamwork. What is that? Teamwork. Cooperating—cooperating with each other when you do something is called cooperation, teamwork, collaboration. Okay? Okay. Alright, today we'll play a game. Son, straighten up. Muzammil, sit down. Right now, there's no use of any pencil. Son, put your pencil down. Put your pencil down. Where is the shortcut now? Okay? Uh, what do you have to do now? You're going to play a game with me. In it, a child will come to me, and I'll whisper a word in his ear. I'll whisper a word in his ear. He has to act on it and tell me what happens. Now you have to act and tell me. Don't say anything out loud, but the kids will guess what you're talking about. Okay? Alright, which child acts well? Someone tell me. Now, Zoha, come here. Alright, Zoha, come here. I'll whisper this into his ear. You have to act on that same thing. Okay? So then all the kids will guess whether you're acting right or not. Right? feedback_loops.feedback_english:
You created a very engaging and clear lesson today, and your students stayed with you throughout the activity.  

**Strengths**  
- **Instructional Clarity**: You gave simple, repeated directions that students could follow, for example, “I will show you some pictures, and you have to guess what that picture is… you have to tell me what that picture is.” This made it easy for them to know what to do.  
- **Logical Flow**: Your questions moved in a clear order, from “What picture is this?” to “What do you understand by looking at it?” and then to more specific prompts like “Which book?” and “What do you study?” This step by step flow helped students build their answers.  
- **Subject Content Accuracy**: You guided students correctly toward “English book” and “storybook,” using true features from the picture like “there are dragons on it” to help them reach the right idea, so they were practicing correct language and concepts.  

You are building a solid foundation for your students with the way you present ideas and guide them to the right answers.
